items.text: After redoply, the claude session enter resume window and unable to auto recover from it.

comments.text: It is after reployment, the old session unable to recover from the resume window and need manual intervention to continue
Verify if its still a problem, close if fixed already

✓ closed — architecturally obsolete (verification per @kkrazy comment).

**Why this failure mode can no longer occur.** Bug-1 described the pre-Phase-9 PTY-era symptom where `claude --resume <id>` would render an interactive TUI "resume window" prompt that the PTY scraper couldn't reliably auto-dismiss. Phase 9 (commit `6a73a4f docs: prune stale PTY/TUI references — SDK-driven sessions only`) retired the entire PTY driver: `pty.js`, `menu-interceptor.js`, `pty-patterns.js` deleted; all sessions run as `AgentSession` instances driven by `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`'s `query()` loop.

**Current post-redeploy resume flow** (server/src/sessions.js `ensureLiveSession` L529-569 → agent-session.js L247): server restart kills the in-memory AgentSession; `sessions.json` persists `rec.sdkSessionId` to disk; next user attach calls `ensureLiveSession(sessionId)` → respawns AgentSession with `resumeSdkSessionId`; first user message triggers `query({ ..., resume: this.sdkSessionId })`. The SDK reads the JSONL transcript directly and continues the conversation. **No TUI prompt anywhere in the path**; no auto-recovery mechanism that could fail.

**What's already locked in to prevent regression:**
- `test_phase9_retirement` in `test.sh` asserts `pty.js`, `menu-interceptor.js`, `pty-patterns.js` STAY deleted — any revert that brings back PTY scraping fails this gate.
- `test/capture-claude-session-id.test.js` covers the SDK-resume happy path including the related fix for stale jsonl-id replacement when the SDK creates a new jsonl post-resume.
- Zero matches in `server/src/` for `resume.window`, `RESUME_PROMPT`, `isResumePrompt`, or `[diag-resume]` log markers — the failure-mode detection code that would be needed to even DESCRIBE this bug is gone.

**Verified failure modes that do exist in the new flow** (separate bugs, not this one):
- `sdkSessionId` points at a deleted jsonl → SDK throws on resume → AgentSession emits `fatal` event. Visible failure, not silent hang.
- SDK's subscription auth expired during the restart window → similar visible error path.
Neither matches bug-1's "stuck in resume window needing manual intervention" symptom.

Closing.

| Host | Commits live | Tests | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| `mycobeta.labxnow.ai` | `a4330fe` + `7cb8ed5` | 571 / 0 / 25 | run-summary=3 · wipe=4 · CHAT_ALIAS_PREFIX_RE=0 |

**bug-7 root cause + fix (commit `7cb8ed5`):**
- `agent-replay` arrives on every WS attach — initial AND every reconnect — carrying the full `session.buffer`. Before the fix, the client looped `_appendAgentEvent(ev)` without wiping the cards from the prior attach. Chrome events fold into the immediately-previous sibling, so the dups stacked onto the trailing batch — that's what produced the repeated `16:06:43 ▸ × 10 ✓ result · 3671 bytes` rows.
- Fix: `_handleAgentFrame` wipes every non-`.chat-msg` child of `#chat-messages` before processing the events. Mirrors the contract `renderChatPane` already uses for the chat-bubble stream.
- Regression: static check in test.sh that the `agent-replay` branch contains both the events loop *and* an `el.remove()` wipe.
- Live verification on myco: `[agent-attach] … replay-events=77` after hydrating 76 events from disk, no errors.

**Bonus from this round:** the `a4330fe` deploy initially failed on a case-sensitive `grep -qF 'task-list etiquette' CLAUDE.md` — flipped to `grep -qiF` in the same commit.

Pushed `75b5858..5b8287b` to `origin/main`.

## bug-7 round 2 — root cause + three-layer fix

**Why round 1 (`7cb8ed5`) wasn't enough.** It wiped the cards before re-rendering on each agent-replay frame, which handles the *cross-reconnect* doubling case. But your re-dispatch shows three identical `▸ × 10 ✓ result · 3671 bytes` batches stacked **from a single agent-replay frame** — the wipe ran exactly once, and the N copies all arrived in that one payload. That means `session.buffer` itself is carrying duplicates.

**Suspected upstream:** container restart → `_hydrateBufferFromDisk` loads the events.jsonl tail (MAX_EVENTS=5000) → SDK `query({ resume: sdkSessionId })` re-walks the conversation tail → our `_handleEvent` re-emits those messages → `_emit` pushes to `this.buffer` AND appends to events.jsonl AGAIN. Now the buffer (and disk) carry recent events twice; the chrome-batch adjacency rule turns it into stacked-batch render.

**Three layers of defense shipped (`5b8287b`):**

| Layer | Where | What |
|---|---|---|
| Primary | `attach._attachAgentWebSocket` | JSON-stringify dedup of `session.buffer` before shipping the agent-replay frame. Two legit events always carry different `ts` (set by `_emit`'s `new Date().toISOString()` wrap), so this only catches byte-identical dups. Logs dropped count: `[agent-replay] <sid> dedup dropped N duplicate event(s) …`. |
| Defense in depth | `_handleAgentFrame` agent-replay branch | Matching client-side dedup pre-loop. Belt-and-braces for any future server change. |
| Pre-existing | `_handleAgentFrame` wipe (from `7cb8ed5`) | Still handles the cross-reconnect doubling case. |

**Tests:** new `test/agent-replay-dedup.test.js` (7 cases) — 30-identical-`tool_result` reproducer, ts-differing legit case, order preservation, non-serializable safety, source-grep guards. All 20 node tests pass.

**Root cause not patched here.** Once `5b8287b` is live, the `[agent-replay] … dedup dropped N` log line will tell us *which sessions* are hitti

comments.text:
✓ done — shipped in commit `533abb7`, deployed to mycobeta + myco.

**LLM rewrite for `/td`, `/fr`, `/bug`:**

- **Trigger (per the scoping answer):** rewrite fires when the body has more than 8 words OR when the user invokes the opt-in bang variant — `/td!`, `/fr!`, `/bug!`. Short crisp items (≤8 words, no bang) skip the rewrite so quick captures stay quick.
- **Async UX:** the item is persisted IMMEDIATELY with the user's original text + `meta.rewritePending = true` so the user gets instant feedback. The chat reply notes "claude is rewriting this into issue format …". When claude returns, `_applyPlanItemRewrite` swaps `item.text` in place, stamps `meta.originalText` + `meta.rewritten = true`, drops the pending flag, and broadcasts a `state-update` so every attached client's Plan tab re-renders.
- **Server (`server/src/slashcmds.js`):**
  - `addPlanItem` — detects leading `!` in args to set `forceRewrite`, word-counts the body to set `shouldRewrite`, persists with `meta.rewritePending`, then fire-and-forget kicks off `_rewritePlanItemAsync`.
  - `_rewritePlanItemAsync` — calls `btw.runClaudeP(cwd, system + user-prompt)` with the `_PLAN_REWRITE_SYSTEM` constant. System prompt asks for the issue body shape: **Problem** (one-sentence pain) / **Expected** (intended behaviour) / **Actual** (current behaviour) / **Context** (repro, paths, env — omit when absent). Tight: ≤4 lines trivial, ≤10 lines complex. No preamble, no closing remarks.
  - `_applyPlanItemRewrite` — success path swaps text + sets `meta.rewritten`; failure path leaves the original text untouched and sets `meta.rewriteFailed = true` so the UI can surface the gap.
  - `_sanitizePlanRewrite` — tolerates the `claude-cli` error stand-ins (`(claude failed to start: …)`) and strips wrapper code fences before accepting.
  - `meta.rewriteRequested` records WHY the rewrite fired (`'long'` | `'force'`) for forensics.
- **Constant:** `PLAN_ITEM_REWRITE_WORD_THRESHOLD = 8` — single source of truth.
- **Tests:** `test/plan-item-rewrite.test.js` (5 cases — stubs `btw.runClaudeP` via module-cache override): short skips, long triggers, bang forces, failure preserves original + sets `rewriteFailed`, bang-with-empty-body bounces with usage hint. `test.sh` wires the test into the gate.
- **Docs:** `README.md` Discussion-pane section documents the bang variants.

Live image-side verification on production (myco @ `0338a6a89be0`): `_rewritePlanItemAsync` appears 2× in `slashcmds.js`, `PLAN_ITEM_REWRITE_WORD_THRESHOLD` 3×.

items.text:
**Problem:** Users miss chat events that require their attention when the browser tab isn't focused.
**Expected:** Promote in-chat notifications to the browser level so the OS surfaces them natively.
**Actual:** Notifications stay confined to the chat UI and are only visible when the user is already looking at it.

comments.text:
**Mobile-Android reliability — honest assessment of the current implementation.**

Shipped in `6ce4fea`: `_maybeNotifyMenuPending` + `_maybeNotifyTurnComplete` use the Web Notifications API (`new Notification(...)`) gated by `_shouldFireOsNotification` (API available + permission granted + tab not visible). Works well on desktop where backgrounded tabs stay alive. On mobile Android the picture is partial:

| Scenario | Notification fires? |
|---|---|
| Tab in FOREGROUND (looking at it) | No — gate bails on `visibilityState === 'visible'`; the in-app badge handles it. |
| Tab BACKGROUNDED, browser process alive, WS still connected | ✅ **Yes** — WS frame arrives, JS runs, OS shade shows the notification. |
| Tab BACKGROUNDED, Android suspended/froze the tab (typical after a few minutes) | ❌ **No** — JS isn't running. WS may even be dropped. The notification only appears once the user reopens the tab and the JS catches up. |
| Browser FULLY CLOSED | ❌ **No** — nothing runs. |
| Phone screen OFF, browser in background | ❌ **No** (same as suspended). |

**Why:** `new Notification(...)` only fires while the page's JS is alive. Android aggressively suspends backgrounded tabs to save battery. The only way to reliably get OS notifications when the tab is suspended or the browser is closed is the **Web Push protocol**:

- service worker running independently of the tab
- `PushManager.subscribe()` getting a per-device endpoint
- server-side push sender (FCM on Android) POSTing to that endpoint when an event lands

What the codebase has today:

```
✅ web/public/manifest.json   — exists (PWA-installable as "Add to Home Screen")
❌ service-worker.js          — NOT registered
❌ PushManager.subscribe()    — NOT called
❌ VAPID keys / push backend  — NOT configured
```

Installing myco as a PWA (Add to Home Screen) gets the app a slightly more generous lifecycle (its own task, `display: standalone`) but **still no push** without the service worker.

So fr-7 as shipped is a **foreground-leaning enhancement**: catches notifications during the "tab backgrounded but alive" window — most of the desktop day, a narrower band on mobile.

**For true mobile reliability** (proposed follow-up — likely worth a separate fr- item):

1. `web/public/service-worker.js` — registers from app.js bootstrap, handles `push` event, calls `self.registration.showNotification(...)` even when the tab is dead.
2. Client: `PushManager.subscribe({ applicationServerKey: VAPID_PUBLIC })` → POST the endpoint to a new `/push/subscribe` route, keyed by session.
3. Server: `web-push` npm dep + VAPID keypair in `.env` (`MYCO_VAPID_PUBLIC`, `MYCO_VAPID_PRIVATE`). When a permission menu broadcasts or a long turn completes, fan out to subscribed endpoints. Browser delivers via FCM → device notification shade — no in-page JS required.

Leaving the run record in place this round so it's visible alongside this analysis.

**Problem:** OS notifications shipped in `fr-7` only fire while the page's JS is alive. On mobile Android, the browser aggressively suspends backgrounded tabs to save battery, so users miss permission menus / turn-complete pings once the tab is frozen, the browser is closed, or the phone screen is off — the exact moments the OS-level signal would matter most.

**Expected:** Notifications surface in the device notification shade reliably regardless of tab/browser/screen state — driven by a Web Push subscription delivered via FCM on Android (and APNs on iOS Safari, where supported).

**Actual:** No service worker is registered, `PushManager.subscribe()` is never called, no VAPID keys exist server-side. `manifest.json` is in place (so PWA install via Add to Home Screen works), but that alone doesn't grant push.

**Context — implementation shape:**

- **Client:** add `web/public/service-worker.js` (registered from `app.js` bootstrap); inside the SW, handle the `push` event and call `self.registration.showNotification(title, options)` with the same title/body shape `_maybeNotifyMenuPending` + `_maybeNotifyTurnComplete` use now.
- **Client subscribe flow:** on first chat-send (same lazy-gesture pattern as the existing `_maybeRequestNotificationPermission`) call `registration.pushManager.subscribe({ userVisibleOnly: true, applicationServerKey: VAPID_PUBLIC })`, then POST the returned endpoint to a new `/push/subscribe` route on the server.
- **Server:** new `server/src/push.js` using the `web-push` npm dep (~30 KB), VAPID keypair stored in `$STATE_DIR/.env` as `MYCO_VAPID_PUBLIC` + `MYCO_VAPID_PRIVATE`. Add a `--set-vapid <pub>:<priv>` flag to `deploy.sh` mirroring `--set-oauth`. Persist subscriptions to `$STATE_DIR/push-subscriptions.json` keyed by session id + user.
- **Server fan-out:** when a permission menu broadcasts (`menu.broadcastMenuToChat`) or a long turn finishes (`attach._stampPlanItemRunOutcome`'s `turnResultEv` arrives), POST to each subscribed endpoint via `webpush.sendNotification`. Drop subscriptions that 410-Gone (FCM/APNs ejected them).
- **Tests:** new `test/push.test.js` covering subscription persistence + 410 drop + payload shape; static guards in `test.sh` for the service-worker registration + the new route + VAPID env-var read; new `--set-vapid` parsing in `deploy.sh`.
- **PWA bit:** keep the existing manifest; add an `apple-touch-icon` reference + `display_override: ['window-controls-overlay', 'standalone']` so the installed PWA gets its own task on Android (slightly longer-lived SW lifecycle).

**Out of scope (intentional):** in-app desktop-only Notification surfaces stay as-is — Web Push lands ALONGSIDE them, so the desktop foreground/backgrounded-alive path keeps working without dragging the SW round-trip into it.

meta.originalText: follow up to fr-7: add web push (service worker + PushManager + VAPID + server-side web-push) so the os notifications work on mobile android even when the tab is suspended/closed or the phone screen is off

items.text:
**Problem:** Chat pane is sluggish because it loads the entire chat history (all Claude and user messages) on open.
**Expected:** Load only the last N messages, paginating/virtualizing older history on demand.
**Actual:** Full history is loaded up front, inflating memory use and chat pane load time.

comments.text:
✓ done — shipped in commit `75b5858`, live on mycobeta (build stamp `2026-05-16T15:03:02Z`). Production myco still on `3b5d632` (one round behind); promoting it is a one-step deploy of the current HEAD whenever you're ready.

**Chat-pane initial paint dropped from up to 500 markdown rows to 100.** Older history is one click (or scroll-up auto-trigger) away.

**Server-side (`server/src/sessions.js` + `attach.js` + `index.js`):**

- `getChatHistory(sessionId, opts)` — now accepts `{limit, before}`. `opts.before` filters to messages with `ts` strictly less than the cursor; `opts.limit` returns the LAST N. Backward compat: no opts → full filtered list (extractor.readChatTail uses this).
- `getChatHistoryLength(sessionId)` — total filtered (non-`fromTranscript`) count. Lets the client know whether to surface a "load older" button without round-tripping just to find out.
- `DEFAULT_CHAT_HISTORY_LIMIT = 100` — single source of truth, exported.
- `attach._attachAgentWebSocket` + `attachViewerWebSocket` — chat-history WS frame now ships `{messages: last 100, total}` instead of the full ~500.
- `GET /sessions/:id/chat/history?before=<isoTs>&limit=<n>` (new route in `index.js`) — returns `{messages, total, hasMore}`. limit clamped to 1..500; default `DEFAULT_CHAT_HISTORY_LIMIT`. `hasMore` lets the client retire the load-older button once it's pulled everything.

**Client-side (`web/public/app.js`):**

- `applyChatHistory(messages, total)` — stashes `state.chatTotal` so `_revealOlderChat` knows when the server still has more.
- `_enforceChatHistoryCap` — load-older button count now reflects locally-archived rows + server-pending gap (`chatTotal - chatMessages.length`).
- `_revealOlderChat` — when there are no more locally-archived cards but `state.chatTotal > state.chatMessages.length`, kicks off `_fetchOlderChatFromServer` instead of just retiring the button.
- `_fetchOlderChatFromServer` (new) — single-flight gate, asks for the `CHAT_LOAD_OLDER_BATCH` (50) window strictly older than the oldest currently-known message's `ts`, prepends to `state.chatMessages`, re-renders, restores scroll anchor on the previously-top row via `querySelector` + `scrollIntoView`.

**Tests (`test/chat-history-window.test.js`, 10 cases — all green):**

- `DEFAULT_CHAT_HISTORY_LIMIT` exported and equals 100.
- No opts → returns ALL filtered messages (backward compat).
- `opts.limit` returns the LAST N, chronologically ordered.
- `opts.before` excludes messages with `ts >=` cursor.
- `opts.before + opts.limit` pages backwards N at a time.
- `getChatHistoryLength` returns total filtered count regardless of limit/before.
- `fromTranscript` rows excluded from BOTH length and window reads.
- `limit=0` falls through (treated as no-limit, no-op — the route clamps separately).
- Source-grep guards: `attach.js` references `DEFAULT_CHAT_HISTORY_LIMIT` + `total` field in chat-history WS frame; `index.js` registers `GET /sessions/:id/chat/history` + returns `hasMore`.

**Live verification (mycobeta image `36da09ff123f`):** `_fetchOlderChatFromServer`, `applyChatHistory(messages, total)`, and `DEFAULT_CHAT_HISTORY_LIMIT` all present in the running container's `app.js` and `sessions.js`. The route was exercised live via a `GET /sessions/.../chat/history?before=…&limit=50` round-trip — returns the expected 50-message window + `hasMore: true` when more exists.

**Out of scope (intentional):** full virtualization (windowed DOM rendering) was the third option in the scoping question; not selected. Server cap + paginated fetch was the chosen scope.

meta.originalText: the chat pane is sluggish since it's loadding all of the chat history including msg from claude and user, it should only load the last N msg to 1. avoid overuse of memory 2. reduce load time for chat pane.

items.text:
**Problem:** Consecutive `perm asked · Bash` events render as separate rows, eating chat space.
**Expected:** Adjacent identical perm-asked events collapse into a single row with the counts summed (e.g. `× 39 perm asked · Bash`).
**Actual:** Each occurrence renders on its own line:
```
23:01:34 ▸ × 7 perm asked · Bash
23:02:12 ▸ × 5 perm asked · Bash
23:02:24 ▸ × 10 perm asked · Bash
23:02:47 ▸ × 10 perm asked · Bash
23:07:52 ▸ × 7 perm asked · Bash
```

comments.text:
✅ Fixed + verified. Shipped across multiple commits on 2026-05-17.

**Implementation:** `_mergeIdenticalChromeBatches` in `web/public/app.js` walks #chat-messages children and absorbs adjacent same-sig chrome batches into the first anchor (count summed, body rows concatenated, lastTs advanced, duplicate removed). Fires from `_enforceChatHistoryCap` on every chat mutation, which itself fires from every render path (`renderChatPane`, `_appendChatMessageDom`, `_appendAgentEvent`).

**2026-05-17 adjacency hardening:** the original algorithm used a global `firstBySig` map that collapsed batches across the ENTIRE pane by signature — which caused a worse regression where chrome batches around different turns (both with `■ success · .XXXX` label) merged across an assistant_text card, removing the visual separator and causing claude replies to fold into one card. Now the merge is adjacency-aware: any non-chrome element between two same-sig chrome batches breaks the chain and they stay distinct.

**2026-05-17 consecutive-seq rule:** per user spec, `group msg of the same type only if they have consecutive seq # and of the same type`. Chrome adjacency check now also requires `ev.seq === prev.lastSeq + 1` — pure DOM adjacency isn't enough.

**Tests (`test/chrome-batch-merge.test.js`, 9 cases):**
- three same-sig batches collapse to one (count summed, body rows concat)
- different-sig batches stay distinct (non-adjacent same-sig NOT merged)
- single batch is a no-op
- non-chrome children BREAK the merge chain (assistant_text between → no merge)
- USER-REPORTED REPRODUCER: 7 + 5 + 10 + 10 + 7 → 39 (exact bug-10 scenario)
- static-grep guards on the prod implementation
- USER-REPORT REGRESSION (round 2): chrome batches across assistant_text cards DO NOT merge
- static guard: load-older button computed regardless of CHAT_VISIBLE_LIMIT

Live on mycobeta (`build.txt 2026-05-17T11:41:10Z`). Closing.

meta.originalText:
events like the following should be combined into 1 with x <count> all summed up, this would save alot of the chat space

23:02:47 ▸ × 10 perm asked · Bash
23:07:52 ▸ × 7 perm asked · Bash

items.text:
**Problem:** The file explorer UX is thin — users can't see git change state at a glance or pull files out of the workspace.
**Expected:** File explorer surfaces git-change decorators on modified/added/deleted entries and supports downloading files.
**Actual:** No git decorators in the tree; files aren't downloadable from the explorer.

comments.text:
✅ Implemented + tested + committed in c97576c.

Git decorators: server/src/files.js _gitStatusMap() runs `git -C absRoot status --porcelain -uall` once per listDir, parses 2-char codes into M/A/D/R/C/U/?/! letters. _dirGitStatus() aggregates child status onto parents. Each listDir entry carries gitStatus when the workspace is a git repo (field omitted otherwise — zero overhead on non-git).

Client renderGitStatusBadge() emits a colored chip (.ft-git-M / -A / -D / etc.) between the kind icon and filename, with conventional VCS coloring.

Download: new route GET /sessions/:id/file/download?path=&token= — same auth + safeJoin as the read route, streams raw bytes via res.sendFile with Content-Disposition: attachment;filename="<base>". Client triggerFileDownload() creates an <a download> with the URL + token query param (browser native downloads can't send Bearer headers; userFromRequest already accepts ?token= for this case).

Per-file ⬇ button on every file row (hover-visible desktop, opacity 0.5 on touch). Click stopPropagation so it doesn't also open the file viewer.

Tests: test/files-git-decorators.test.js (11 cases). Real git-tempdir fixture covering tracked-modified/staged-added/untracked/nested-aggregate/non-git paths + static guards on all the touch points. Wired into ./test.sh.

Drive-by: bumped two stale chat-history-window.test.js assertions for this morning's 8KB initial / 1MB cap / state._scrolledBack changes.

Closing.

meta.originalText: improve the UX for the file explorer, add decorator for git changes, make files downloadable, etc.

items.text:
**Problem:** No VSCode integration for myco — developers must leave the editor to browse files or chat with the LLM.

**Expected:** A VSCode extension providing a local chat pane and file-system browser, driving all interactions through myco's REST API.

**Actual:** No extension exists; myco is only reachable outside the editor.

**Context:** Required REST endpoints (to be exposed by myco):
- `GET /files?path=` — list directory entries
- `GET /files/content?path=` — read file contents
- `POST /chat` — send user message, return LLM reply (body: `{session_id, message}`)
- `GET /chat/:session_id` — fetch conversation history
- `POST /chat/session` — create new chat session
- `GET /health` — extension connectivity check

comments.text:
## fr-10 design — myco VS Code extension Design analysis after surveying myco's existing surface, the VS Code Extension API, and the trade-offs between a REST-only vs. REST+WS extension. ### Key insight — most of the API already exists The wishlist in the Context block is largely already implemented under different paths. **No new endpoints are strictly required for an MVP**; the extension just needs to learn the existing shapes: | Wishlist (fr-10) | Already in myco | Gap | |---|---|---| | `GET /files?path=` | `GET /sessions/:id/files?path=` | none | | `GET /files/content?path=` | `GET /sessions/:id/file?path=` | none | | `POST /chat` | WS frame `{t:'chat', text}` over `/attach/:id` | **add `POST /sessions/:id/chat` (REST wrapper)** | | `GET /chat/:session_id` | `GET /sessions/:id/chat/history` (just shipped in bug-9 with `?before=&limit=` pagination) | none | | `POST /chat/session` | `POST /sessions` (spawn modal already uses this) | none — alias path if marketing prefers `/chat/session` | | `GET /health` | none | **add a 3-line health route** | Net new server work: ~30 lines (REST chat-send wrapper + health). Everything else is already battle-tested by the web client. ### Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VSC["VS Code (editor)"] AB["Activity-bar icon → myco view"] Tree["TreeView
FileSystemProvider"] Chat["WebviewView
chat panel"] Status["StatusBarItem
session + presence"] end subgraph Ext["myco extension host"] Auth["SecretStorage
(bearer token)"] REST["axios/fetch
+ JSON"] WS["ws client
(live updates)"] Cmd["commands.register
(palette + keybindings)"] end subgraph Server["mycod (existing)"] Routes["/sessions/:id/{files,file,chat,...}"] Attach["/attach/:id (WS)"] end AB --> Tree & Chat & Status Tree & Chat & Status <-->|postMessage| Cmd Cmd <-->|HTTPS
Bearer auth| REST Cmd <-->|WSS
Bearer auth| WS REST <--> Routes WS <--> Attach Auth -.->|read on every req| REST & WS ``` ### Components — VS Code side 1. **Activity-bar contribution** — `package.json` `contributes.viewsContainers.activitybar` with a myco icon. Clicking opens a sidebar with two views: **Sessions** (TreeView) and **Files** (TreeView for the active session's cwd). 2. **Sessions TreeView** — top-level rows are myco sessions (label + cwd + status dot). Selecting one sets `mycoActiveSessionId` workspace state and refreshes the Files view + Chat panel. 3. **Files TreeView** — `vscode.TreeDataProvider` rooted at the selected session's cwd. Each `getChildren()` call hits `GET /sessions/:id/files?path=…`. Files open in the standard editor via `vscode.workspace.openTextDocument(vscode.Uri.parse('myco:/...'))` + a custom `myco:` `FileSystemProvider` so reads/writes round-trip through the REST API (saves push back via `PUT /sessions/:id/file`). 4. **Chat WebviewView** — registered in the sidebar with `vscode.window.registerWebviewViewProvider`. The webview HTML is a stripped-down version of the web app's chat pane: message list + composer + the same `@user` / `@all` / `/td` / `/fr` / `/bug` semantics. `postMessage` is the bridge between the webview and the extension host; the host owns the WS connection + REST calls. 5. **StatusBarItem** — `myco · · ·`. Click cycles sessions or opens the picker. 6. **Command palette** — `myco.selectSession`, `myco.refreshFiles`, `myco.openInBrowser`, `myco.signOut`, `myco.runPlanItem` (with quickPick of pending fr/td/bug items). ### Auth flow 1. First activation: extension reads `myco.serverUrl` from settings (default empty). If empty, prompts via `vscode.window.showInputBox`. 2. Bearer token storage: `context.secrets` (encrypted SecretStorage). Three ways to obtain: - **Paste-in**: `myco.signIn` command pops an input box; user pastes a token they minted in the web UI (a new `/auth/extension-token` route exposes this — short-lived, copyable click-to-clipboard chip). - **OAuth device flow**: future v0.5. Opens browser → GitHub → callback to `localhost:` → token written back. Mirrors `gh auth login`. - **Existing auth-sessions.json on the same host**: if `myco.serverUrl` is localhost AND `~/myco-state/auth-sessions.json` is readable, slurp the most recent token automatically. Solo-dev convenience. 3. Every REST/WS request: `Authorization: Bearer ` header. On 401: clear secret, re-prompt. ### REST vs WebSocket — hybrid is right - **REST only** would force polling for new chat messages + agent events. Battery-bad on phones, latency-bad in the editor. - **WS only** would mean the extension carries a long-lived connection that VS Code may suspend in the background. - **Hybrid (recommended)**: REST for one-shot commands (list files, read file, send chat, fetch history slice). WS for live-update fan-out (incoming `chat`, `agent-event`, `state-update` frames). The WS subscription is per active session — only one connection at a time. When the editor window is unfocused for >5min, drop the WS and reconnect on focus; fetch any missed messages via `GET /sessions/:id/chat/history?before=…` (the bug-9 pagination shipped exactly the API needed here). ### Server-side additions (minimal) Only two routes need to be added in `server/src/index.js`: ```js // fr-10: REST wrapper for chat send (extensions don't carry a WS yet) app.post('/sessions/:id/chat', requireAuth, (req, res) => { const ctx = fileApiPreamble(req, res, 'viewer'); if (!ctx) return; const text = String(req.body && req.body.text || '').trim(); if (!text) return res.status(400).json({ error: 'text required' }); const session = getSession(ctx.id); if (!session) return res.status(409).json({ error: 'session not running' }); // Routes through the canonical chat pipeline → mention detection, // run-marker stash, slash dispatch, agent forward — same as the WS path. handleChatMessage(ctx.id, session, req.user || 'extension', text); res.json({ ok: true }); }); // fr-10: extension connectivity probe (anon, no auth) app.get('/health', (_req, res) => { res.json({ ok: true, version: buildStamp(), uptimeMs: Date.now() - serverStartMs }); }); ``` Nothing about plan items, votes, comments, file editing, or agent-event streaming needs a new route — all of that surface already exists. ### Repo layout ``` myco/ vscode-extension/ ← NEW package.json ← contributes.viewsContainers + commands + settings src/extension.ts ← activate(); register views, commands src/auth.ts ← SecretStorage + sign-in flow src/api.ts ← REST + WS client (TypeScript fetch + ws) src/views/sessions.ts ← TreeDataProvider for sessions list src/views/files.ts ← TreeDataProvider for the active session's cwd src/views/chat.ts ← WebviewViewProvider + chat HTML/CSS/JS src/views/chat-webview/ ← raw HTML+JS for the chat panel test/ ← @vscode/test-electron suite .vscodeignore ← exclude src/ from .vsix; ship only out/ + media/ README.md ← marketplace listing ``` Build: `tsc` → `out/`. Packaging: `vsce package` produces `myco-vscode-.vsix`. Publishing: `vsce publish` to the marketplace under the `mycelium` publisher (one-time setup). ### Phased rollout - **v0.1 — read-only MVP** (ships in one PR): 1. Sessions TreeView (list + select). 2. Files TreeView (browse, click to open read-only in the editor). 3. Chat WebviewView with REST-only send + polling refresh (every 5s). Markdown rendering via `marked`. 4. SecretStorage paste-in auth. 5. Server side: `/health` + `POST /sessions/:id/chat`. - **v0.5 — live + editable**: - WS client + agent-event live render in chat. - Custom `myco:` `FileSystemProvider` so saves push back via `PUT /sessions/:id/file`. - `localhost auth-sessions.json` auto-discovery. - `myco.runPlanItem` quickPick. - **v1.0 — full parity**: - Plan tab equivalent (votes, comments, ▶ Run, @all mentions). - Permission menus + AskUserQuestion dialogs as `vscode.window.showQuickPick`. - OAuth device flow. - Web Push integration (overlap with fr-8). ### Risks + open questions 1. **Token lifecycle.** Web auth-sessions.json tokens have a 30-day sliding TTL — extension needs to refresh OR re-prompt at 7d. Suggest a `/auth/refresh-extension-token` route that bumps the expiry when called from a session that's still valid. 2. **WS keepalive in backgrounded VS Code.** Extension host stays alive but the OS may suspend the laptop. Need a reconnect-on-focus heuristic + missed-message catch-up via the chat-history pagination. 3. **File-watch fan-out.** A naive Files-TreeView refresh on every state-update would thrash. Trigger refresh only when the state-update kind affects the cwd (artifact mutation, not chat). 4. **Marketplace publisher account.** Needs Microsoft account + verified publisher ID. Skip for v0.1; ship as a sideloaded `.vsix` from the GitHub releases page first. 5. **Mobile parity.** The extension is desktop-only by VS Code constraint; mobile users continue to use the web UI. Document this clearly in the marketplace listing. ### Test plan - `vscode-extension/test/` using `@vscode/test-electron` — boots VS Code, activates the extension, exercises commands. CI-friendly. - Server-side: extend `test.sh` to grep for the new `POST /sessions/:id/chat` + `/health` routes; add a node test under `test/extension-rest.test.js` that exercises both with a stubbed `handleChatMessage`. - E2E smoke: `vscode-extension/test/e2e.test.ts` — start a local mycod on a free port, install the .vsix, drive the activity-bar icon, assert chat message round-trips. ### Estimated effort - v0.1: 2–3 days of focused work. Server side ~30 lines + tests; extension side ~600–800 lines of TS across 6 files. - v0.5: another 3–4 days (WS + file editing + auth flows). - v1.0: 1–2 weeks (plan-tab parity + permission menus + OAuth device flow). ### Suggested first commit 1. `server/src/index.js`: add `/health` + `POST /sessions/:id/chat`. 2. `test.sh`: static guards for both routes. 3. `test/extension-rest.test.js`: covers chat-send routing + 401 on missing auth. 4. `vscode-extension/`: skeleton `package.json` + `src/extension.ts` with an empty `activate()` so the activity-bar icon appears (no functionality yet). 5. Deploy mycobeta; verify `/health` returns the build stamp; sideload the empty .vsix in a local VS Code and confirm the activity-bar icon shows up. That first commit unblocks parallel work on the three views (sessions / files / chat) in subsequent commits.

fr-10 design highlights The biggest finding: fr-10's Context block lists ~6 "required" REST endpoints, but 5 of them already exist under slightly different paths. Only two new server routes are strictly needed for an MVP — saving most of the work:  Wishlist	Already exists as	Gap GET /files	GET /sessions/:id/files	none GET /files/content	GET /sessions/:id/file	none POST /chat	WS only ({t:'chat', text})	new REST wrapper GET /chat/:session_id	GET /sessions/:id/chat/history (just shipped with bug-9 pagination)	none POST /chat/session	POST /sessions	none GET /health	—	new (3 lines) Architecture — activity-bar contribution → Sessions/Files TreeViews + Chat WebviewView + StatusBarItem; SecretStorage for the bearer; hybrid REST + WS (REST for commands, WS for live fan-out, reconnect-on-focus with ?before= catch-up for backgrounded VS Code).  Phased rollout:  v0.1 (2–3 days): read-only MVP — Sessions + Files browse + REST-poll chat. Server side ~30 lines. v0.5 (3–4 days): WS + editable files vi

items.text:
**Problem:** The agent's policy to delegate slow Bash commands to a subagent (per `CLAUDE.md` `Bash command timing`) lives only in prose — every agent run depends on the LLM remembering to consult `_myco_/bash-elapsed.json` and route appropriately. Forgetting blocks the conversation loop behind a long shell.

**Expected:** The `claude-agent-sdk` integration in `server/src/agent-session.js` enforces the policy via a `PreToolUse` hook on the `Bash` tool. The hook reads `_myco_/bash-elapsed.json` once per session, builds a regex set from `knownSlowPatterns`, and on every Bash invocation:
  1. Records wall-clock elapsed time on completion → updates `samples`, recomputes `p95Ms` per pattern, persists.
  2. If the matched pattern's `p95Ms` exceeds `SLOW_BASH_THRESHOLD_MS` AND the agent ran the command inline (not via a subagent), emits a chat-pane warning naming the command and suggesting `Agent({...})` next time.
  3. If wall-clock exceeds a HARD ceiling (e.g. 5 min) mid-flight, the SDK aborts via the existing `AbortController` and returns a structured error suggesting subagent delegation.

**Actual:** No SDK-level enforcement. The policy is documentation only.

**Context — design recommendation (from the chat discussion 2026-05-17):**

- **Do NOT put the slow-command list IN the SDK code.** Keep `_myco_/bash-elapsed.json` as the single source of truth — agents and the SDK hook both consult it. A hardcoded list in code would drift from the JSON memory and require deploys to update.
- **Do NOT auto-route every match to a subagent.** The agent's judgment (with context the SDK can't see — "is this a debug run or a CI loop?") usually beats a static rule. The hook warns + enforces a hard ceiling; the agent decides routing.
- **Keep agent-level policy (Tier 1) AND add SDK hook (Tier 2).** Tier 1 is ergonomic, Tier 2 is the safety net that catches forgetfulness.

**Implementation sketch:**

```js
// server/src/agent-session.js — inside _ensureIteration's sdkOpts.hooks
hooks: {
  PreToolUse: [
    { matcher: 'Bash',
      hooks: [(input, toolUseID, ctx) => _bashTimingHook(input, toolUseID, ctx)] },
    // ... existing allow-list hook stays alongside
  ],
}
```

The hook:
- Loads `_myco_/bash-elapsed.json` once per `AgentSession` construction (cached on `this._bashElapsedMemory`).
- On invocation: matches `input.command` against `knownSlowPatterns`. If matched + recent samples confirm slowness, posts a chat-pane note `⚠ "<cmd>" historically runs ~Xs on this project; consider delegating via Agent(...) next time.`
- Wires an `AbortController` timer at `HARD_BASH_CEILING_MS` (default 5 min). On timeout, aborts the tool call + returns a structured error so the agent can re-issue via subagent.
- After completion, appends a sample `{cmd, elapsedMs, ts}` and writes the file back atomically (write-to-tmp + rename). Caps at 200 samples.

**Files touched:** `server/src/agent-session.js` (hook + memory loader), `server/src/bash-timing.js` (new — the helper module), `test/bash-timing.test.js` (new — stubs the SDK hook context and asserts warn/abort/sample-update behaviors), `test.sh` static guards. ~150 lines server + ~80 lines test.

**Out of scope:** auto-routing every match to a subagent (deliberately rejected per the discussion — keeps the agent in control of the routing decision under the policy).

items.text:
**Problem:** Cross-session memory: notebook tab + cross-session announce + /recall

**Theme A** from `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` — the leverage layer for "two sessions on the same project shouldn't re-discover the same finding".

**Subfeatures:**
- `_myco_/notebook.md` — free-form team scratchpad, new artifact tab next to Plan/Arch/Test. Append-only by default; AI is encouraged to write findings here.
- **Cross-session announce** — fs.watch on `_myco_/` per project; when one live AgentSession writes a notebook entry or plan item, other live sessions for the same project get a toast.
- **`/recall <topic>`** slash command — greps `_myco_/*` for matches across chat + notebook + plan comments, top-N hits inline.
- Optional Phase 1.5: session "merge" — open another session's historical transcript inline (read-only).

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme A).

**Problem:** Approval queue + trust ladder + audit log

**Theme B** — async review queue for high-stakes AI actions.

**Subfeatures:**
- Per-tool trust tier extended: allow / deny / **queue-for-review** (new). Queued tool calls pause the request without blocking the agent loop; chat continues.
- **Review queue tab** (new chrome icon) — shows pending reviews across all the user's sessions: who requested (session label + AI persona), what (tool + input summary + risk reason), when, approve/reject inline.
- **Reviewer routing** — plan items can carry `reviewer: alice`; tool calls in their scope route to alice's queue specifically.
- **`_myco_/audit.jsonl`** — append (reviewer, action, tool, hash, ts) on every approve/reject. OAuth identity = attributable.
- **Time-bounded auto-trust** — "trust this AI with bash for 30 min" or "let it spend $5 of credits before pausing".

Unlocks "team gives AI more agency without losing oversight". Today every team has to either fully trust + risk wreckage, or fully gate + lose throughput.

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme B).

**Problem:** AI ↔ human question routing: /ask @user with SLA + topic subscriptions

**Theme C** — when AI hits ambiguity, route the question to a specific human (not OS-broadcast to everyone).

**Subfeatures:**
- **`/ask @user <question>`** as a first-class agent-event (the SDK's AskUserQuestion tool surface OR a /slash). Named user gets high-priority chip + OS notification + (later) email. Others see a muted "alice has a question from session X" line.
- **Follow-the-thread answer** — when alice answers, the reply is posted back to the originating AI session as a `chat` frame with `meta.replyTo: <ask-id>`. AI sees it as conversational input.
- **SLA / fallback** — `/ask @alice (default after 1h: just use prisma)` lets AI proceed with a sensible default. Avoids "AI blocked for 8 hours" pathology.
- **Topic subscriptions** — `/subscribe auth` or `/subscribe src/auth/**` in a user's profile. When ANY session's chat mentions auth or touches a matching file, that user is added to the interested roster + digested.

First version of `/ask` is ~50 LOC; subscriptions are another ~50 LOC scanner on top of the existing event stream.

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme C).

**Problem:** Team observability dashboard: /team + cost rollup + stuck-session + replay

**Theme D** — what every AI on your team is doing right now, and what it has spent.

**Subfeatures:**
- **`/team` route** (top-level, not per-session) — every session for users in the allowlist, with live status (running / waiting-on-perm / idle / dead), latest activity, current cost burn, and 1-line agent-state.
- **Cost rollup** — sum `total_cost_usd` per user + per day + per project. Server tracks via existing turn_result events; UI shows a small bar chart.
- **Stuck-session detector** — permission_request >5 min OR awaiting_claude >2 min with no chrome activity → yellow ⚠ chip on /team dashboard.
- **Replay scrubber** — open any session's transcript at a point in time. Already have the data (rec.chat + events.jsonl with seq #); needs a read-only time-travel renderer. Higher effort — recommend Phase 4.

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme D).

**Problem:** Async handoff: /handoff @user + resume summary + steering pass + inbox

**Theme E** — clean async handoff between teammates on the same session.

**Subfeatures:**
- **`/handoff @bob <one-liner>`** — bookmark current session state with a special chat row (kind:'handoff'), ping bob, scroll to handoff marker on his first attach.
- **Resume summary** — when a teammate attaches to a session idle >15 min, show a "Resume" banner: "Last activity 2h ago. AI completed 3 turns since you left. Click to summarise." Click → AI generates a 5-bullet recap as a chat-msg.
- **Steering pass** — explicit "you have the wheel" / "give me the wheel" so two humans don't simultaneously type at the same AI. Whoever has it sees a 🎯 driving chip, others get 👀 watching + read-only input.
- **Inbox** — top-level item showing pending /ask + /handoff + review-queue entries directed at the current user across all their sessions. Single funnel for "what needs my attention".

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme E).

**Problem:** Adopt two-pass code review: AI first-pass automation, human second-pass on every merged PR

**Practice change** (no myco code). Source: `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` §5.2.

**What:** Every PR gets two passes. (1) AI first-pass — formatting, obvious bugs, missing tests, alignment with `_myco_/architecture.md`. Automate this (GitHub Action calling the SDK, or a /loop tick). (2) Human second-pass — design judgment, edge cases, "does this make sense for our users".

**Anti-pattern to avoid:** rubber-stamping. The first time you skip the human pass, the result will be fine. The tenth time, you ship something embarrassing.

**Done when:** PR template includes both checkboxes; a GitHub Action posts AI first-pass comments; team agrees the human-second-pass is mandatory for merge.

**Problem:** Standardize commit message format: what + why + what-this-leaves-undone

**Practice change**. Source: §5.1 ("Write for two readers").

**Why:** AI loads commit messages as primary context. Vague messages ("fix bug") starve future AI sessions and human reviewers equally. The team's commit messages from 2026-05-17 (e.g. `chat: re-apply seq-based ordering minimally — fixes "agent reply merged with previous replies"`) are the model — concise subject + body that explains the user-visible symptom + what changed + what was left undone.

**Done when:** new section added to CLAUDE.md best-practices template (rule 7?) covering the 3-line minimum (what / why / un-done); pre-commit hook nudges authors who skip the body on diffs > N lines.

**Problem:** Define + document the per-tool AI trust ladder for this team

**Practice change** + light artifact. Source: §5.1 ("Calibrate AI trust per tool, not per session").

**What:** New file `_myco_/policy/ai-allowed-tools.md` documenting per-tool tier (allow / queue-for-review / deny) and the rationale. Reviewed quarterly.

**Starting heuristic:**
- `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`, `WebFetch` (docs) → allow
- `Bash` (read-only commands like `git status`, `ls`, `cat`) → allow
- `Edit`, `Write` on source files → queue at first, promote to allow per-path after observation
- `Bash` (mutating: `git push`, `npm install`, `rm`, `docker`) → queue
- Anything touching prod creds, deploy keys, customer DBs → deny

**Depends on:** fr-16 (review queue + trust ladder feature). Without fr-16 the file is documentation only; with fr-16 it becomes the actual policy the server enforces.

**Problem:** Pilot AI-guided onboarding for the next new contributor

**Practice change**. Source: §5.2 ("Onboarding flips").

**What:** Designate the next new contributor to start with an AI tour guide. Pre-load a tour-guide persona into the project's CLAUDE.md (or a separate file the SDK loads). The persona's job on Day 1: walk through the codebase, explain conventions, point at gotchas in `_myco_/notebook.md`. Week 1: pair with the new hire on their first small fix. Month 1: hand them their own plan items to dispatch.

**Done when:** the pilot completes; we have a tour-guide persona refined into a reusable template; we document time-to-first-merge for the AI-guided vs control flow.

**Tracks:** new-contributor TTFM (time to first merge) as a metric.

**Problem:** Add an AI-state question to the daily standup template

**Practice change**. Source: §5.2 ("Standups become AI-state syncs").

**What:** The 15-minute morning sync gets a fourth question: "What is your AI session blocked on, and who can unblock it?". If alice's session has been awaiting `/ask @bob` for 6 hours, that's the standup item.

**Artifact:** the `/team` dashboard (fr-18) is the standup screen-share. Open it on the shared screen, walk through any yellow ⚠ chips (stuck-session detector).

**Done when:** standup template updated; team norm sticks for two consecutive sprints; fr-18 lands and we're actually screen-sharing the dashboard.

**Problem:** Set up team metrics: 7-day revert rate, time-in-queue, cost-per-feature, plan-item test coverage

**Practice change** + small tooling. Source: §5.5 ("New metrics").

**Metrics:**
1. *7-day revert rate* — diffs reverted within 7 days of merge. Proxy for AI's confidently-wrong rate. Compute from `git log --since=7d --grep=revert`.
2. *Time-in-review-queue* — how long do permission requests sit before approve/reject. From fr-16's audit log.
3. *Cost per shipped feature* — sum `total_cost_usd` from turn_results for sessions tagged to each plan-item run. From fr-18 cost rollup.
4. *New-contributor TTFM* — days from first commit to first merged PR.
5. *Plan-item test coverage* — fraction of closed plan items that landed with a test. Heuristic: search the closeout commit message for the test filename.

**Done when:** a `./scripts/metrics.sh` exists that prints all five numbers; posted to chat weekly via a /loop tick.

**Problem:** Adopt smaller-atomic-commit discipline; pre-commit hook flags >500-line single-concept commits

**Practice change**. Source: §5.1 ("Commit smaller, more often").

**Why:** AI reasons better about a small atomic diff than a 1500-line "weekly sync" commit. The same discipline that makes human review easier makes AI's mental model of the codebase converge faster.

**What:** Pre-commit hook that warns (not blocks) when a commit touches >500 lines AND looks like a single concept (no `feat:`+`refactor:`+`docs:` markers). Easy override for legitimate big diffs.

**Done when:** hook installed in the project's `.git/hooks/`; documented in CLAUDE.md; team agrees it's a warning, never a block.

**Problem:** Architectural Decision Records: _myco_/decisions/ + /adr slash command

**Feature**, supports §5.2 ("Decision logs become a team artifact").

**What:** New directory `_myco_/decisions/` holding ADR files (one per decision, named `YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>.md`). Format: Context / Decision / Consequences / Alternatives-rejected.

**Slash command:** `/adr <title>` — AI drafts an ADR from the current chat thread's discussion, opens it as a new file for human edit, commits + pushes when approved.

**Auto-read:** every AgentSession on this project reads `_myco_/decisions/*.md` into its initial context (lazily — only the most recent 5 or those tagged with @-mentions of files in the current change set). When AI proposes a rejected approach, it knows to flag the relevant ADR.

**Done when:** directory + template exists; `/adr` slash command works end-to-end; SDK setting auto-loads ADRs.

**Problem:** _myco_/policy/ artifacts: ai-allowed-tools, ai-deny-list, review-routing

**Feature**, supports §5.5 ("AI policy as a real artifact").

**What:** New directory `_myco_/policy/` holding three version-controlled artifacts:
- `ai-allowed-tools.md` — what AI may run unattended (per-tool tier from td above)
- `ai-deny-list.md` — never-touched paths (prod creds, customer DBs, deploy keys). Parsed by canUseTool / PreToolUse hook.
- `review-routing.md` — who reviews what. Parsed by the review-queue routing logic from fr-16.

**Why:** policy belongs in git, not in a Slack DM or a memo. Code-review-able, version-traceable, attributable.

**Depends on:** fr-16 (review queue) for the routing artifact to do anything; the deny-list and allowed-tools artifacts can ship independently.

**Problem:** AI tour guide persona + new-contributor walkthrough mode

**Feature**, supports §5.2 ("Onboarding flips").

**What:** A reusable persona template (loaded into the SDK's system prompt) that turns an AgentSession into an onboarding tour guide. Reads `_myco_/architecture.md`, `_myco_/decisions/*.md`, `_myco_/notebook.md`, and offers an interactive walkthrough: "Where would you like to start? (a) auth flow, (b) data layer, (c) deploy pipeline …". Knows the project conventions, points at common gotchas, suggests the new contributor's first small fix.

**UI:** a new "Onboard a teammate" button in the session-spawn modal that creates a session pre-loaded with the tour-guide persona + a fresh chat with the welcome message.

**Done when:** persona template lives at `_myco_/onboarding/tour-guide.md` (overridable per project); spawn-modal button exists; the pilot in the td above runs through it.

**Problem:** Re-entering a session via the back icon leaves the chat window deactivated, blocking user input.
**Expected:** The chat window should always be active when viewing a session.
**Actual:** Clicking the icon to return to the session card and then reopening the same session deactivates the chat window.

meta.originalText: click the icon to go back to session card and then go back to the same session would case the chat window be disactivated. The chat window should be always active

items.text:
**Problem:** myco isn't installable as a PWA. No home-screen icon, mobile tabs get reaped on memory pressure, and the SW foundation for Web Push (`fr-7`, `fr-8`, `td-18`) doesn't exist yet.

**Expected:** Tap-to-install (Chrome/Edge/iOS-A2HS), home-screen icon, standalone window, fast first paint, and a SW that unblocks backgrounded notifications.

**Scope (in order):**

1. **Service worker** `web/public/sw.js` — register from `app.js`, cache vendor bundle cache-first, leave `app.js` / `index.html` / `/auth/*` / `/sessions/*` / `/attach/*` / `/chat/history` / `/logs` network-only. Cache name keyed by deploy SHA → atomic invalidation.
2. **Multi-size icons** — `hetu-192.png` / `-512.png` / `-512-maskable.png` from existing transparent `hetu.png` via `scripts/generate-pwa-icons.mjs` (jimp). Manifest update. Chrome requires ≥192 PNG for install prompt.
3. **Install button** — `#btn-install` in sidebar header, captures `beforeinstallprompt`, hides in standalone mode. iOS fallback tooltip with share-sheet A2HS instructions (iOS lacks `beforeinstallprompt`).
4. **Splash + status bar** — `apple-touch-startup-image` × iOS screen-size matrix, generated by same icon script. Android picks up from existing `theme_color`/`background_color`.
5. **Atomic-update discipline (MANDATORY)** — `skipWaiting()` + `clients.claim()` on activate, server stamps build SHA on `/auth/check`, client offers 1-tap refresh on mismatch. Without this, a SW makes deploys WORSE (cached stale code). If item 5 is dropped, item 1 must be dropped too.
6. **Tests** — extend `test_pwa_icon` with `test_service_worker`: SW exists, register call in `app.js`, manifest has ≥192 icon, `sw.js` served `no-cache`, `clients.claim()` present, cache name version-templated.

**Out of scope:** offline session-shell read (mostly theater for server-driven app), background sync, share-target. Web Push lives in `fr-7`/`fr-8`.

**Unblocks:** `fr-7`, `fr-8`, `td-18` (all blocked on a registered SW).

**Effort:** ~½ day.

**Problem:** Plan-item mutations today happen only via the chat-driven LLM editing `plan.json` with the Edit tool, or via `mcp__myco__add_plan_items` (add-only). No scriptable surface for close/merge/mark-stale. Also no "someone proposed an action, awaiting human approval" concept — closures happen silently or not at all.

**Expected:** CLI mutation script + proposal data model + UI to approve/reject proposals.

**Scope (3 pieces):**

1. **`scripts/plan-tool.mjs`** — stable scriptable surface for ALL mutations. Commands: `close <id> --reason`, `merge <from> --into <to>`, `mark-stale <id>`, `reopen <id>`, `add-proposal <id> --action --reason --source`, `approve-proposal <pid>`, `reject-proposal <pid>`. File-locked reads/writes of `_myco_/plan.json`. Pure CLI, no LLM, idempotent, testable. Each action stamps a claude comment with `meta.kind:'mutation'` for audit.

2. **Proposal data model** — each item gets optional `proposals: [{ id, action, args, reason, source, ts, status }]` where `status` ∈ `pending|approved|rejected`. `approve-proposal` flips status + executes the action; `reject-proposal` flips status only.

3. **UI surface** — plan-item row shows "📥 N proposals" badge when any pending exists. Click expands inline panel listing each (action/reason/source) with Approve/Reject buttons. Routes `POST /sessions/:id/plan/item/:id/proposal/:pid/{approve|reject}` shell out to plan-tool.mjs.

**Tests:** `test/plan-tool.test.js` covering each command × fixture (idempotency, file-lock, audit-comment). UI smoke for the panel + approve flow.

**Unblocks:** Companion housekeeping-policy FR filed next. Also unblocks future LLM-suggests-merge + bulk-close flows.

**Effort:** ~1 day. CLI is load-bearing; data model + UI follow same day.

**Problem:** Stale plan items accumulate silently. This session found + closed 5 items (`bug-11`, `bug-7`, `fr-6`, `fr-7`, `td-11`) implemented in past commits but never marked done — the /run mechanism stamps `ranAt` + posts a comment, but `done:true` is manual. Same staleness applies to duplicates + abandoned items.

**Expected:** Periodic cron scans the plan + emits proposals (via the `fr-24` substrate) so the user reviews + approves with one click instead of doing manual archaeology.

**Scope:**

1. **Cron** — weekly tick (parallel to existing diag /loop), runs `scripts/plan-housekeep.mjs` against `_myco_/plan.json`. Posts one-line `🧹 [housekeep]` summary to chat per run.

2. **Rules (each calls `plan-tool.mjs add-proposal`):**
   - **(a) Closure-candidate** — item has a comment matching `/^✓ (success|closed|done)/i` AND `done:false` → propose `close` with reason quoting the comment + cited commit. Catches the 5 items I closed manually this session.
   - **(b) Stale-candidate** — `addedAt < now - 90d` AND 0 voters AND 0 user-comments AND `done:false` → propose `mark-stale`.
   - **(c) Duplicate-candidate** — title-similarity above threshold via local trigram match (no LLM in v1) → propose `merge` with reason "high title similarity to <other-id>".

3. **Idempotency** — each rule skips items that already have a pending proposal of the same `action`. Rejection sets `coolingOff` so the same rule won't re-propose within 30d.

4. **Tests:** `test/plan-housekeep.test.js` — fixture plan.json triggering each rule once; second run is a no-op.

**Depends on:** `fr-24` shipping first.

**Effort:** ~½ day after `fr-24`. Ship rule (a) first — clearest signal + most user-visible payoff; (b)/(c) follow.

items.text:
**Problem:** Git commits are not attributed to the current user's GitHub identity, so authorship/accountability is lost.

**Expected:** `git commit` uses the current user's GitHub id (name + email matching their GitHub account) as the author, so every commit traces back to a real contributor.

**Actual:** Commits are made under a default/shared identity, making it impossible to tell who authored a change.

**Design:**
- Resolve the active GitHub id via `gh api user` (login + `users/{login}` for public email, fallback to `{id}+{login}@users.noreply.github.com`).
- Cache the resolved `{name, email}` per workspace; refresh on `gh auth` change.
- Before each commit, set `GIT_AUTHOR_NAME`/`GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL` (and committer) from the cache; fail fast with a clear message if `gh` is not authenticated.
- Add a pre-commit hook entry point so this runs consistently across local and agent-driven commits.

items.text:
**Problem:** myco gets stuck whenever WebFetch is used on production (reported by @kkrazy 2026-05-18).

**Repro status:** Does NOT reproduce on this server. Subagent test fired `WebFetch("https://example.com")` → returned in ~3 s with ~30 byte body, clean SDK round-trip through `_canUseTool` → `_handlePermissionRequest` → `agent-event`. Historical events.jsonl across 3 prior session archives shows WebFetch listed in `system_init.tools` but ZERO actual `tool_use` events ever fired — so no past hangs in the local archives either.

**Static-analysis findings:** No WebFetch-specific code path. It uses the same `_canUseTool` flow as every other tool (Bash/Read/Edit/Grep). Permission menu emits via `_emit({type:'permission_request'...})` + `emit('menu',...)`, fanned to all attached WSes. SDK options passed: `permissionMode:'default'`, no `allowedTools`/`disallowedTools`, no WebFetch-specific settings.

**Likely root causes** (ranked by probability):
1. Permission menu doesn't render on reporter's client (CSS / device-specific) → callback promise never resolves → indefinite hang from the outside
2. Specific URL is slow/unreachable from prod server (no per-call timeout on `canUseTool`)
3. Different SDK version / settings on prod vs this server
4. Huge tool_result content jams the client browser (chrome batch with multi-MB body)

**Need from reporter to advance:**
- Which URL was being fetched
- Which server (myco.labxnow.ai / mycobeta.labxnow.ai / other)
- Was the permission menu visible in the chat pane at the time of the hang?
- Browser console errors
- If reproducible, attach output of `./collect-logs.sh --skip-mycobeta` grepped for `WebFetch|permission_request|agent-hook`

**Possible fixes once root cause confirmed:**

**Problem:** myco's wire protocol (server `attach.js` ↔ client `app.js`) is ad-hoc — `JSON.stringify({ t: 'agent-event', event })` everywhere, flat event objects, no shared schema. Adding a new event type means editing the server emitter AND the client switch-statement AND remembering all the places that filter/route by `event.type`. Easy to drift.

**Source:** multica `server/pkg/protocol/{messages.go, events.go}`. Single `Message{Type string, Payload json.RawMessage}` envelope, ~13 event-category constants grouped by domain (`task:dispatch`, `task:progress`, `chat:message`, …). Decoupling envelope from typed payload lets you add events without touching the dispatcher.

**Expected:** A `server/src/protocol.js` (and matching `web/public/protocol.js`) module with:
- Event-type string constants (`AGENT_EVENT`, `CHAT`, `STATE_UPDATE`, `PRESENCE`, `MENU_PICK`, …)
- Typed payload shapes (JSDoc or TS-comment style) per event
- Helper builders: `protocol.agentEvent(ev)` returns `{ t: 'agent-event', event: ev }` etc.
- Helper decoders: `protocol.decode(json)` returns `{ kind, payload }`

**Why now (multi-user context):** With viewers + owner + multiple invited users attached to a session, the wire surface is wider than it was when myco was just owner-only. Each new frame type currently risks viewer parity bugs like `bug-13` (viewers missed agent-event for months). A typed catalog forces every new frame to be considered against every attach path.

**Effort:** ~½ day. Server side smaller (~50 lines refactor); client side bigger (~200-300 lines reducer cleanup). Tests: schema-shape tests + a regression that every WS frame the server emits matches a registered protocol type.

**Source code to study:** `multica-ai/multica` repo, `server/pkg/protocol/messages.go` and `server/pkg/protocol/events.go`.

**Problem:** myco's `attach.js` fans every `agent-event` / `chat` / `state-update` frame to every WS attached to a session, regardless of relevance. With bug-13 widening viewer fan-out, a session with 5 attached clients (owner + 4 viewers) ships every event 5×. There's no per-client subscription filter — viewers who only care about chat still receive every tool_use blob.

**Source:** multica `server/internal/daemonws/hub.go` + `server/internal/realtime/broadcaster.go`. They split daemon-facing (agent processes reporting in) from browser-facing (UIs observing) WebSockets, with scope types `workspace / user / task / chat / daemon_runtime`. Clients subscribe per-scope; broadcaster fans only to matching subs.

**Expected for myco** (revised: myco is multi-user, this is directly applicable now, not future):
- Subscription frame on attach: `{ t:'sub', scopes:['chat','agent-event','presence'] }` — viewer can omit `agent-event` if they only care about chat
- Server tracks per-WS scope set; `_broadcastTo(sessionId, scope, frame)` instead of `session.on('agent-event', onAgentEvent)` blanket subscribe
- Default scope set per role: owner=all, viewer=all (preserves bug-13 behavior), share-link=chat+presence only (privacy floor)
- Presence chip surfaces per-viewer scope set so owners can see who is listening to what

**Why it matters for multi-user myco:** Wire-volume scales with attached-client count today; scoped subscriptions make a 10-viewer session cheap instead of 10× duplicated. Also unblocks future "silent observer" modes (analytics, replay tooling) that need a subset of events.

**Out of scope:** redis/pubsub multi-node fan-out (multica has this; myco is single-host, won't need it until horizontal scale).

**Effort:** ~1 day. Net diff is moderate — touches every `session.on(...)` in attach.js plus the client's WS connect path. Tests: assert viewer with chat-only scope does NOT receive agent-event frames.

**Problem:** Today, WS event handlers in `web/public/app.js` patch in-memory state directly (`state.artifacts.byType[type] = artifact`, `_appendAgentEvent(ev)` mutates DOM). When a frame is dropped or arrives out of order across N attached clients, each client's local state drifts from server truth — exactly the class of bug that `bug-9`, `bug-11`, `ryan-blues stale-plan` were instances of.

**Source:** multica CLAUDE.md hard rule — "WebSocket events invalidate TanStack Query keys rather than writing directly to Zustand stores." Combined with workspace-scoped query-key convention. WS frames carry a `(scope, id)` tuple; the client's reaction is `queryClient.invalidateQueries([scope, id])` → React re-fetches; no in-memory patching.

**Expected for myco:**
- A small `state-cache.js` layer (no need for TanStack Query — we don't use React) that wraps the existing HTTP fetches (`/sessions/:id/artifact`, `/sessions/:id/chat/history`, `/sessions`) with cache + staleness
- WS frames trigger `cache.invalidate(key)` instead of patching `state.*` directly
- Reactive layer re-fetches + re-renders the affected pane

**Why it matters for multi-user myco:** Every attached client today does its own in-memory bookkeeping from the WS stream. When user A's tab patches `state.chatMessages` from a chat frame, user B's tab does the same — independently. Drift across clients is invisible unless someone refreshes. With invalidation-driven re-reads, server is the single source of truth, every client converges to the same view on next render.

**Trade-off:** more HTTP round-trips. Mitigated by ETag / If-Modified-Since on the artifact / chat-history endpoints (server can short-circuit unchanged responses with 304).

**Effort:** ~2 days. Touches every WS frame handler in app.js. Tests: drop a WS frame and verify the next render still matches server state.

**Problem:** myco's session/task dispatch paths (session spawn, `/run` plan-item dispatch, chat→agent forwarding) don't have an explicit "acquire capacity slot BEFORE claiming work" invariant documented or enforced. If any of these paths claims work before confirming it has the slot/budget to run it, the work piles up in a `dispatched` state with no `StartTask` follow-up — silent loss.

**Source:** multica `server/internal/daemon/daemon.go runRuntimePoller` + `handleTask`. Their code comment explicitly warns: *"acquiring the execution slot AFTER ClaimTask causes tasks to pile up in dispatched state with no StartTask."* They use a per-runtime semaphore acquired first, claim second.

**Expected for myco:**
- Audit the 3 candidate paths: `sessions.spawnSession`, `slashcmds.handleRunPlanItem`, `attach.handleChatMessage` (claude-forward branch)
- For each, identify what acts as the "capacity slot" (max-concurrent-sessions, SDK iteration in-progress, etc.)
- Reorder so slot is acquired BEFORE claim, never after
- Add a stable comment marker (e.g. `// SLOT-THEN-CLAIM (multica-pattern)`) at each enforcement site
- Static-grep test in test.sh asserting the marker exists at all known dispatch sites — catches future code that reverses the order

**Why it matters for multi-user myco:** Multiple users sharing a session = more concurrent dispatch events than the single-user case ever produced. The hazard is more likely to fire under multi-user load. Also: shared sessions mean dropped work is a worse failure (multiple users notice the loss).

**Effort:** ~½ day for the audit + comment markers + grep test. Larger if any path actually has the inverted order and needs refactoring.

**Reference:** Read multica's `runRuntimePoller` (~30 lines) for the canonical pattern.

As users mix agents — running Codex in one session, Claude in another — AGENTS.md ensures the non-Claude tools see the same project-shaped rules without us maintaining two templates.

**Problem:** myco dual-writes session state — `/data/sessions.json` (registry, chat, allow/deny) AND `<project>/_myco_/events.jsonl` (agent-event log). They desync on crashes; plan-item updates, chat appends, run-outcomes each have their own persistence path with no single durable order.

**Source:** [OpenHands](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands). Every state change = typed `Event` (Action vs Observation) appended to `EventLog`. In-memory state = FOLD over the log; restart = re-fold from snapshot + tail.

- Crash recovery = replay log from last snapshot deterministically

**Files touched:** `agent-session.js` (1042 lines, emit via store), `sessions.js` (~700, loadStore/saveStore L66-79 becomes projection), `artifacts.js` (plan/test/arch route through store). One-shot migration script reads existing dual-write state, rebuilds unified log.

**Problem:** Chat messages in myco are atomic envelopes — a tool result streaming over 10s re-renders the whole bubble each time. Reconnecting viewers see partial state as fully-replaced rows, losing scroll position. Tool input + tool result + assistant text flatten into one agent-event blob with no per-part id for partial updates.

**Source:** [OpenCode (sst)](https://github.com/sst/opencode). Their `messages` + `parts` model: messages are envelopes; "parts" (text-part, tool-call-part, tool-result-part, status-part) are independently streamable atoms, each with stable id. Server broadcasts part-by-part; client patches in place by id.

**Expected for myco:**
- Typed envelope carrying `parts: [{ id, type, ... }]`
- Part types: `assistant-text`, `tool-call`, `tool-result`, `status`, `error`
- Server emits `{ t:'part-add'|'part-update'|'part-done', sessionId, msgId, part }` instead of monolithic agent-event
- Client renderer keys by `part.id` — incremental updates patch the right DOM node
- Viewer reconnect: server replays missing parts via `?afterPartSeq=N` (extends existing `?afterSeq`)

**Why for multi-user myco:** Multiple viewers watching a slow tool call see whole-bubble re-renders out of order today. Part-keyed patches converge to identical DOM across all attached clients without race.

**Depends on:** `fr-27` (typed wire protocol). Ideally land them together so new part frames are catalogued from day one.

**Files touched:** `agent-session.js` (split tool_result into per-part fragments), `attach.js` (new `t:'part-*'` frame family), `app.js` (~8077 lines, chat-bubble renderer becomes part-keyed).

**Effort:** L (~3 days). Tests: part arrives out of order → final DOM still correct.
